problem_statement: Coding agents introduce unintended edits, subtle bugs, and scope drift that slip past code review, forcing developers to decide how much autonomy to grant; static permission settings and instruction files cannot track developers' shifting, context-dependent autonomy preferences over time.
magnitude_evidence: 81% of users preferred intermediate checkpoints in a recent 48-participant study [12]; formative survey found subtle bugs that pass review (13/21), erosion of codebase understanding (12/21), security vulnerabilities (10/21) as leading concerns
insufficiency_evidence: Claude Code with CLAUDE.md 'ask before API-shape changes' caught only 2 of 4 required check-ins (recall 0.50), silently skipping two signature-change operations despite explicit instruction; without a rule (CC-B) recall 0.00; 'Auto Memory stores preferences as prose but does not produce a quantitative check-in threshold'
solution_mechanisms: dynamic autonomy calibration from interaction traces
online logistic-regression policy engine over 13-dim action features
two-tier governance: hard constraints vs behavioral guidance
three-tier approval cascade with proceed/flag/check-in thresholds
retrieval of past corrections into prompt context
legible session summary separating policy- vs agent-initiated check-ins
